summary: SH-3 understand gate: does the A7 leaked worker on system_long_wq race module-unload (UAF)? Does the 1200ms budget close it? Was the 20:52 wedge this? Design the SH-3 live test + safety.
logs: Understand done. UAF verdicts: a7-life=YES — a genuine rmmod-path timeout is a ; kernel-wq=YES — a genuine rmmod-path timeout is a

a7life.topic: A7 (F40b) shutdown-worker lifecycle and leaked-worker UAF semantics
findings: STRUCT (nv.c:2179-2186): struct nv_f40b_shutdown_work is heap-allocated via kzalloc(sizeof(*w), GFP_KERNEL) at nv.c:2228. It embeds struct work_struct work, struct completion done (heap-resident), the rm_call function pointer, sp, nv, and atomic_t refcount. Both the work struct AND the completion live in this single heap allocation, so the data survives until the last work_put frees it; the FUNCTION CODE it points to (rm_call -> rm_shutdown_adapter, plus nv_f40b_shutdown_worker itself) is module TEXT, not heap.

REFCOUNT MODEL: atomic_set(&w->refcount, 2) at nv.c:2243 ('one ref for caller, one for worker'). Two drops via nv_f40b_shutdown_work_put (atomic_dec_and_test -> kfree, nv.c:2188-2192): the worker drops its ref at the end of nv_f40b_shutdown_worker (nv.c:2201) AFTER w->rm_call() returns and complete(&w->done); the wrapper drops the caller ref unconditionally at nv.c:2291 after the wait returns. Last-to-zero frees the struct.

POINT 1 (COMPLETE-WITHIN-BUDGET, SH-1 ~600ms < 1200ms): queue_work(system_long_wq, &w->work) at nv.c:2249, then wait_for_completion_timeout(&w->done, msecs_to_jiffies(timeout_ms)) at nv.c:2251. Worker runs w->rm_call() (rm_shutdown_adapter) ~600ms, then complete(&w->done) (nv.c:2200) which wakes the wrapper, then work_put (refcount 2->1, nv.c:2201). wait returns jiffies_left>0, 'completed within budget'. Wrapper then work_put at nv.c:2291 (1->0 -> kfree). Ordering note: the worker's complete() at line 2200 happens BEFORE the worker's own work_put at line 2201, so when the wrapper wakes, rm_shutdown_adapter HAS returned. There is a benign micro-race on which of the two work_puts runs last (the worker's put at 2201 vs the wrapper's at 2291), but rm_shutdown_adapter is fully done before the wrapper proceeds. No leaked worker on this path; struct freed; no module-unload hazard.

POINT 2 (TIMEOUT, OLD 200ms < 600ms): wait_for_completion_timeout returns 0 (jiffies_left<=0). Wrapper takes the else branch (nv.c:2259-2289): logs 'worker leaked', calls C5 sink rm_cleanup_gpu_lost_state(sp, nv, NV_GPU_LOST_DETECTOR_AER_FATAL) at nv.c:2273, counts the fire via nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired_teardown() at nv.c:2288, then work_put at nv.c:2291 drops the caller ref (refcount 2->1, struct NOT freed). Wrapper returns to nv_shutdown_adapter and the rest of teardown proceeds. The worker is STILL executing w->rm_call() = rm_shutdown_adapter (RM closed code, chip-touching MMIO) and still holds 1 ref. When it eventually returns it does complete() (no waiter now) and work_put (1->0 -> kfree).

MODULE REFERENCE: NEITHER the wrapper NOR the worker takes any module reference. grep across all of kernel-open/nvidia/*.c finds NO try_module_get/__module_get on the F40b path (the only try_module_get in the tree is nv-dma.c:969 on a GEM dma-buf fops->owner, completely unrelated). The work runs on system_long_wq, which is an extern struct workqueue_struct * declared in the KERNEL's include/linux/workqueue.h:463 (verified against /lib/modules/7.0.9-204.fc44.x86_64/source) — a SHARED kernel-owned workqueue, NOT created by nvidia.ko (no alloc_workqueue/create_workqueue for it). A work item queued on a shared kernel workqueue does NOT pin the module that supplied the work function. So module_ref_held_by_worker = NO.

NO UNLOAD DRAIN: There is NO flush_work / cancel_work_sync / drain_workqueue / flush_workqueue targeting the F40b shutdown work item anywhere on the teardown path. grep for flush/cancel referencing f40b/shutdown_work returns EMPTY. The cancel_work_sync at nv-pci.c:2408 (tb_egpu_recover_stop) is for the A3 recovery reset_work (st->reset_work, nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:873), a DIFFERENT work item. The two cancel_delayed_work_sync at nv-pci.c:1440/1447 are for tegra boost_disable. nv_module_exit (nv.c:693-705) and nvidia_exit_module (nv.c:1047) do nv_module_state_exit + rm_shutdown_rm + resource frees but never flush system_long_wq. So unload_drains_workqueue = NO.

POINT 3/4 (UAF mechanism): The leaked worker, after the wrapper returns, is mid-rm_shutdown_adapter (module text) and the work function nv_f40b_shutdown_worker (nv.c:2194) is itself module text. The heap struct survives (it carries the live ref), but the CODE both functions execute is nvidia.ko .text. nv_shutdown_adapter is invoked from nv_pci_remove_helper (nv-pci.c:2556) — the PCI .remove path that runs under rmmod for a bound device. If rmmod proceeds to completion and the kernel frees nvidia.ko's text/data while the leaked worker is still running rm_shutdown_adapter, the worker executes freed text -> use-after-free / panic. Nothing in the code prevents this: no module pin, no unload-time drain. The design relies ENTIRELY on the C5 sink (rm_cleanup_gpu_lost_state) making subsequent sink-aware MMIO inside RM fail-fast so the worker exits 'quickly' — a best-effort race, not a guarantee. If the worker is wedged on a chip MMIO that does NOT consult the sink (PCIe Completion Timeout in RM closed code with no sink check at that point), it can outlive the module text free.

BUDGET CONTEXT: live apnex.22 NVreg_TbEgpuShutdownTimeoutMs = 1200 (passive read of /sys/module/nvidia/parameters), default in source is 1200 (nv.c:2165). With 1200ms budget vs measured ~600ms graceful completion, the COMMON case completes within budget (worker done, no leak). The leaked-worker UAF window only opens on a genuine timeout (worker still in rm_shutdown_adapter at budget expiry), which the bumped budget makes rare but does NOT eliminate (the 200->1200 bump narrows, not closes, the window).

/lib/modules/7.0.9-204.fc44.x86_64/source/include/linux/workqueue.h:463 — extern struct workqueue_struct *system_long_wq (kernel-owned shared wq, not nvidia-created)
/root/open-gpu-kernel-modules/kernel-open/nvidia/nv.c:693 — nv_module_exit (no system_long_wq flush/cancel)
/root/open-gpu-kernel-modules/kernel-open/nvidia/nv.c:1047 — nvidia_exit_module (no system_long_wq flush/cancel)
a7life.uaf_verdict: YES — a genuine rmmod-path timeout is a real use-after-free hazard. On timeout, the wrapper returns and lets teardown/rmmod proceed while the worker is still executing rm_shutdown_adapter (module text) and nv_f40b_shutdown_worker is itself module text. The worker holds NO module reference (no try_module_get/__module_get on the path; system_long_wq is a shared KERNEL-owned workqueue that does not pin nvidia.ko), and nothing on the unload path (nv_module_exit, nvidia_exit_module, nv_pci_remove_helper) flushes or cancels the F40b system_long_wq work item. The heap struct survives via its retained refcount, but if rmmod completes and the kernel frees nvidia.ko's text before the leaked worker returns, the worker executes freed code -> UAF/panic. The C5 sink (rm_cleanup_gpu_lost_state) is a best-effort 'make MMIO fail-fast so the worker exits' mitigation, NOT a synchronization barrier — it does not guarantee the worker has left module text before unload. The 200->1200ms budget bump (live apnex.22 = 1200) makes the timeout rare (common ~600ms case completes within budget, worker fully done, struct freed, no hazard) but does not architecturally close the window. To make it safe would require either pinning the module for the worker's duration (try_module_get/module_put) or an unload-time cancel_work_sync/flush of the F40b work on system_long_wq — neither exists today.
open_questions: Does rm_shutdown_adapter's chip-touching MMIO path actually consult the C5 sink (PDB_PROP_GPU_IS_LOST) at the point where it would wedge on a PCIe Completion Timeout? If not, the leaked worker can block for the full PCIe CTO duration regardless of the sink, widening the UAF window. (This is SH-2's territory: eBPF on osDevReadReg032.)

On a real rmmod, does the .remove path actually reach module text-free fast enough to overlap a leaked worker, or does later teardown (kthread_q_stop, rm_shutdown_rm) serialize long enough that the worker always finishes first in practice? Empirically unverified — the 20:52 wedge suggests the worker can outlive, but the exact ordering vs module text free is not proven from source alone.

Should A7 take try_module_get at queue time + module_put in the worker (pin nvidia.ko for the worker's lifetime), OR add cancel_work_sync(&w->work) on the F40b work at module/pci-remove exit? cancel_work_sync would re-introduce an unbounded block (the exact hang A7 exists to bound), so module-pin is the cleaner fix — but module_put from a leaked worker after the wrapper returned needs the struct ref to stay valid (it does, via refcount). This is the likely A9/A7-followup design.

The benign micro-race between the worker's work_put (nv.c:2201) and the wrapper's work_put (nv.c:2291) on the success path is correctly refcounted, but worth a one-line confirmation that complete() at 2200 cannot be reordered after the worker's put — it can't (sequential within the worker), so the wrapper always sees rm_shutdown_adapter done before it proceeds.

kernelwq.topic: SH-3: Does the rmmod path drain/flush/cancel the A7 nv_f40b_shutdown work item queued on system_long_wq before nvidia.ko text is freed? (use-after-free analysis)
findings: A7 architecture confirmed: nv_f40b_shutdown_bounded (nv.c:2204) queues a per-call work struct (struct nv_f40b_shutdown_work, allocated by kzalloc, refcount=2) onto the GLOBAL system_long_wq via queue_work() at nv.c:2249, then wait_for_completion_timeout for NVreg_TbEgpuShutdownTimeoutMs. On timeout it sets the C5 sink (rm_cleanup_gpu_lost_state) + counts the fire, then calls nv_f40b_shutdown_work_put(w) which only drops the CALLER's ref. The worker keeps its own ref and may still be executing w->rm_call == rm_shutdown_adapter (the worker fn nv_f40b_shutdown_worker, nv.c:2194, runs rm_call then complete() then put). This is a deliberate, documented LEAK: nv.c:2264 'worker leaked, will exit when MMIO fails-fast post-sink-set'.

system_long_wq is a global shared kernel workqueue NOT owned by nvidia.ko: declared 'extern struct workqueue_struct *system_long_wq;' in include/linux/workqueue.h:463, allocated/owned by kernel/workqueue.c. The nvidia module never creates or owns it, so the module's exit path would never (and does not) call flush_workqueue/destroy_workqueue on it. The refcount=2 keeps the WORK STRUCT (w, a kzalloc heap object) alive across the worker's run, but does NOT keep the MODULE TEXT (the worker function code) alive.

EXHAUSTIVE flush/cancel sweep of the entire kernel-open/nvidia/ tree: ZERO occurrences of flush_workqueue / flush_scheduled_work / destroy_workqueue / drain_workqueue anywhere. The only cancel_work_sync is A3's tb_egpu_recover_stop on '&st->reset_work' (nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:873) — a DIFFERENT work struct on a different queue, not the A7 shutdown work. The only cancel_delayed_work_sync are Tegra boost_disable (nv-pci.c:1440/1447), unrelated. NOTHING anywhere cancels or flushes the nv_f40b_shutdown_work / nv_f40b_open_work items on system_long_wq.

The A7 work struct is local/anonymous: w is heap-allocated inside nv_f40b_shutdown_bounded and never stored in nvl or any module-global registry, so there is no handle by which any teardown code COULD cancel_work_sync it even if it wanted to. Contrast: A3 stores its work in nvl->recover->reset_work and therefore CAN cancel it; A7 deliberately does not, by design (the leak-and-fail-fast strategy).

rmmod call chain confirmed end-to-end: module_exit(nvidia_exit_module) (nv.c:6986) -> nvidia_exit_module __exit (nv.c:1047) -> nv_drivers_exit (nv.c:656) -> nv_pci_unregister_driver (nv-pci.c:3076) -> pci_unregister_driver(&nv_pci_driver) (nv-pci.c:3082). pci_unregister_driver invokes .remove == nv_pci_remove (nv-pci.c:3063) -> nv_pci_remove_helper(pci_dev, true) which calls nv_shutdown_adapter (nv-pci.c:2556), the site of the A7 bounded wrapper. After nv_shutdown_adapter RETURNS (having timed out and left the worker running), nv_pci_remove_helper proceeds to nv_dev_free_stacks, NV_KFREE(nvl) (nv-pci.c:2585), rm_free_private_state, release_mem_region, etc. — tearing down exactly the nv_state_t/nvidia_stack_t that the leaked worker's rm_shutdown_adapter still references.

No synchronization barrier exists between nv_shutdown_adapter returning and the leaked worker finishing. pci_unregister_driver / device_release_driver do NOT wait for in-flight work on a shared workqueue (shared-wq work is not associated with the driver/device). After nv_pci_remove returns, pci_unregister_driver returns, nv_module_exit and the rest of nvidia_exit_module run, and the kernel's free_module() frees the module's .text/.data. free_module() only waits for things tied to the module (synchronize_rcu, its own refcount reaching 0) — it does NOT scan system_long_wq for work whose function pointer lands in the module. So free_module can free the text of nv_f40b_shutdown_worker / rm_shutdown_adapter WHILE that code is still executing on a system_long_wq kworker.

Module refcount does NOT protect against this. Exhaustive grep: the only try_module_get/module_put in the tree is nv-dma.c:969/999 on a GEM importer's fops->owner (DMA-buf path) — entirely unrelated. The F40b worker functions do NOT call try_module_get(THIS_MODULE). delete_module(2)/free_module gates on module->refcnt, but the leaked worker never bumped it, so a busy worker is INVISIBLE to the unload refcount check. (THIS_MODULE appears only at nv.c:255 as a struct .owner field, not a live ref taken by the worker.)

Practical mitigation in the live build narrows but does not close the window: NVreg_TbEgpuShutdownTimeoutMs was bumped 200->~2000ms (apnex.22, 1200ms budget per task brief; SH-1 found rm_shutdown_adapter busy-polls ~600ms then completes). With a ~1200ms budget vs ~600ms typical completion, a HEALTHY rmmod completes the worker within budget so no leak occurs on the common path. The UAF is reachable only on a GENUINE timeout (worker still wedged in rm_shutdown_adapter past the budget, e.g. PCIe Completion Timeout MMIO hang on a recovered eGPU chip) — which is precisely the scenario the wrapper exists to survive, and precisely when the leak fires.

/root/open-gpu-kernel-modules/kernel-open/nvidia/nv-dma.c:969 (only try_module_get in tree — GEM fops->owner, unrelated to F40b workers)
kernelwq.uaf_verdict: YES — a genuine rmmod-path timeout is a real use-after-free of the module text (and of nvl/nv_state_t). On timeout, nv_f40b_shutdown_bounded leaves the worker running rm_shutdown_adapter on the GLOBAL system_long_wq and returns. Nothing on the unload path waits for it: (1) system_long_wq is a kernel-owned shared workqueue, never flushed/cancelled/destroyed by nvidia.ko (exhaustive sweep: zero flush_workqueue/cancel_work_sync targeting the A7/A6 work struct — the work struct is anonymous/local and untracked, so it is not even cancelable); (2) pci_unregister_driver/device_release_driver do not wait for in-flight work on a shared wq; (3) the worker never took a module refcount (try_module_get), so delete_module/free_module's refcount gate does not see it. Consequently free_module() can free the text of nv_f40b_shutdown_worker and rm_shutdown_adapter while the leaked kworker is still executing them, AND nv_pci_remove_helper NV_KFREEs nvl (nv-pci.c:2585) while the worker still dereferences w->nv/w->sp — a double UAF (freed code + freed state). The refcount=2 on the work struct keeps only the small heap 'w' alive; it does NOT pin the module text or nvl. The risk is confined to the genuine-timeout case (worker wedged past the ~1200ms budget); the C5-sink fail-fast is a best-effort RACE to make the worker exit before free_module runs, not a guaranteed barrier — there is no synchronization actually preventing the worker from outliving free_module. Mitigation that would CLOSE it: track the work struct in nvl (as A3 does) and cancel_work_sync it before NV_KFREE(nvl)/module exit, or queue on an owned workqueue that is destroy_workqueue'd on exit, or have the worker hold a module ref. None of these is currently present.

forensics.topic: Re-examination of the 2026-05-29 20:52 rmmod-path wedge under the SH-1 finding (rm_shutdown_adapter completes ~600ms; the old 200ms budget leaked a worker)
findings: DECISIVE: A7 (the shutdown-path bounded-wait wrapper that creates the leaked worker) was NOT deployed in the 20:52 boot. The kernel log build banner is 'NVIDIA ... 595.71.05 ... (root@nvidia-driver-injector-s7bxd) Fri May 29 09:46:52 UTC 2026' = aorus.18-f40b, which contains ONLY the A6 open-path wrapper. grep across the entire 4.5k-line kernel log and 9.6k-line all-log finds ZERO shutdown-path F40b lines: no 'rm_disable_adapter scheduled to bounded worker', no 'rm_shutdown_adapter scheduled', no shutdown 'completed within budget', no shutdown 'timed out'. The A7 intent provenance confirms A7 first deployed in aorus.19 at 21:35 wall-clock — ~40 min AFTER the 20:52 wedge. Therefore there was NO leaked worker at 20:52: the rmmod ran the VANILLA synchronous nv_shutdown_adapter (rm_disable_adapter + rm_shutdown_adapter called inline on the teardown thread, no queue_work).

The leaked-worker UAF mechanism (H-SH5) requires A7's queue_work(system_long_wq) to have run and a worker to still be executing at module-text-free. Since A7 was absent, the precondition for the UAF simply did not exist in this boot. The UAF hypothesis is mechanistically inapplicable to the 20:52 event — it can only apply to A7-bearing builds (aorus.19+).

Module-unload ordering (nv.c:1047 nvidia_exit_module): nv_drivers_exit() -> nv_pci_unregister_driver() (nv-pci.c:3082) -> nv_pci_remove per device -> nv_pci_remove_helper -> nv_shutdown_adapter (nv-pci.c:2556) [the would-be worker site] -> nv_dev_free_stacks (2557) -> NV_KFREE(nvl) (2585). THEN nv_module_exit(sp) -> rm_shutdown_rm -> nvlink_drivers_exit -> nvlink_core_exit -> emits 'Unregistered Nvlink Core' (nvlink_linux.c:409). So the 20:52:18 nvlink-unregister line is emitted near the END of nvidia_exit_module, AFTER per-device nv_shutdown_adapter has already completed. By 20:52:18 the chip-touching teardown MMIO was already done and the module-exit function was essentially finishing; kernel frees module text right after nvidia_exit_module returns (~20:52:18).

Timeline does NOT fit a worker-completion UAF even hypothetically. The forensics records: rmmod's nvlink-unregister at 20:52:18, new pod veth at 20:52:26, freeze at 20:52:28 (last log line 'cni0: port 17(vethe092bfe0) entered forwarding state'). A hypothetical 200ms-budget leaked worker would finish rm_shutdown at ~rmmod-time+600ms — i.e. it would race the module-text-free within <1s of 20:52:18, producing a UAF/panic at roughly 20:52:18-19, NOT 10s later at 20:52:28. The observed +10s gap points to a LATER touch tied to the NEW pod's container setup (BPF program loads, veth/cni0 creation, netfilter/iptables-restore, avahi mDNS), not to the teardown worker's tail.

No workqueue drain protects the (hypothetical) worker on the unload path. There is NO flush_workqueue(system_long_wq) / drain_workqueue / cancel_work_sync for the A7/A6 worker anywhere in nv_pci_remove or nvidia_exit_module. The only cancel_work_sync (nv-pci.c:2408, tb_egpu_recover_stop) is for the A3 recovery work item, a DIFFERENT mechanism. So on A7-bearing builds the UAF race is genuinely unguarded by code structure (relevant to SH-3 for aorus.19+, but moot for the 20:52 event).

Neither A7 nor A6 worker takes a module reference: nv_f40b_shutdown_worker (nv.c:2122-2130) and nv_f40b_open_worker (nv.c:~1840) call rm_call/nv_open_device... then complete()+put(); no try_module_get/__module_get/THIS_MODULE refcount. A worker holding a module ref would BLOCK rmmod (module ref-count nonzero) and thus prevent the unload-vs-worker race entirely; because it does NOT, on A7-bearing builds a genuinely-still-running worker cannot stop the module text from being freed -> the UAF would be real THERE. But this is the SH-3 question for aorus.19+, not the 20:52 event.

Best-fit hypothesis for the 20:52 wedge is (b)/(d): a DIFFERENT event during the new pod's container setup at +8s to +10s. The freeze coincides exactly with the new pod (sj492/vethe092bfe0) reaching cni0 forwarding state. The original forensics already noted the new pod's nvidia.ko build/load hadn't started (~50s lead time), so it was the container's kernel-subsystem interactions (PCI sysfs reads, an async PCI/AER scan, a TB-tunnel poll), or a modprobe/PCI-rescan touching a chip left in a bad post-rmmod substrate state, that triggered a chip-touching MMIO hang. This is consistent with the broader F40/surprise-removal/modprobe-spin classes documented in memory, NOT with a teardown-worker UAF.
code_citations: /var/log/mission-1-archaeology/a7-deploy-wedge-2026-05-29/journalctl-kernel-wedge.log:2317 (build banner: 595.71.05 root@nvidia-driver-injector-s7bxd Fri May 29 09:46:52 UTC 2026 = aorus.18-f40b)

forensics.uaf_verdict: The 20:52 wedge does NOT fit the leaked-worker UAF hypothesis, on two independent grounds. (1) PRECONDITION ABSENT: A7 (the patch that schedules the leaked worker) was not in the 20:52 build — the kernel log shows the A6 open-path wrapper but ZERO shutdown-path F40b lines, and the build banner (aorus.18-f40b, Fri May 29 09:46:52 UTC) predates A7's first deploy in aorus.19 by ~40 min. The 20:52 rmmod ran the vanilla synchronous nv_shutdown_adapter, so no worker was ever queued and none could be leaked. (2) TIMING MISMATCH even hypothetically: a 200ms-budget leaked worker would finish rm_shutdown at ~rmmod+600ms and thus race module-text-free within <1s of 20:52:18, firing a UAF panic at ~20:52:18-19 — NOT at the observed 20:52:28 (+10s). The +10s gap aligns precisely with the NEW pod's container-setup phase (BPF loads, veth/cni0, netfilter, avahi), so the wedge points to a SEPARATE chip-touching event during pod startup on a chip left in a bad post-rmmod state (best fit: hypothesis (b) a different F40-class event during container setup, or (d) modprobe/PCI-rescan touch), NOT the teardown-worker UAF. NET: the original forensics attribution ('F40-class wedge on the rmmod path, rm_shutdown_adapter chip-touching MMIO hangs') is itself now undermined by SH-1 (rm_shutdown_adapter completes in ~600ms, does not hang), and the SH-1-era UAF refinement does not rescue it for THIS event. The UAF is a real, unguarded risk for A7-BEARING builds (aorus.19+: no try_module_get on the worker, no system_long_wq flush on the unload path) and remains the correct SH-3 question — but it is not what happened at 20:52.
open_questions: SH-3 (for A7-bearing aorus.19+): with no module reference and no system_long_wq flush on the unload path, can a genuinely-still-running ~600ms rm_shutdown worker survive past NV_KFREE(nvl)/nv_dev_free_stacks (UAF of sp/nv) and past module-text-free (UAF of the worker function pointer)? Needs a dedicated rmmod-path experiment. Note: with the apnex.22 1200ms budget on a HEALTHY chip, rm_shutdown completes in ~600ms WITHIN budget on the caller thread (no worker leaked at all), so the UAF window only opens on a GENUINE >1200ms timeout — much rarer than the old 200ms guillotine.

What actually caused the 20:52 freeze at +10s? Candidates: (b) an async PCI/AER scan or TB-tunnel poll touching the post-rmmod chip during the new pod's setup; (d) a modprobe/PCI-rescan; or a container PCI-sysfs read. The kernel froze before logging it; pinning requires bpftrace through an rmmod+pod-restart cycle on a reproduction (the forensics itself flagged this as unpinnable from the journal alone).

Was the chip in an F40-precondition (bad substrate) state at 20:52 such that a benign post-rmmod kernel touch could hang? The forensics' long-idle period (20:03-20:51) and the earlier 19:55 nv_init_msix ioremap WARNING are circumstantial; substrate state at rmmod time is unverified.

Does the A7 intent (and the 'A7 is load-bearing on every rmmod' framing) need a provenance correction? Its Purpose still cites the 20:52 forensics as the originating attestation, but that event (a) ran without A7 and (b) is now doubly-undermined (SH-1 says rm_shutdown doesn't hang; this analysis says no leaked worker existed). The A7 v1.2 header already flags the reframing; the Purpose/Provenance prose may overstate the 20:52 link.

Should A7 (or a follow-on) add try_module_get in the worker and/or a flush_workqueue(system_long_wq) in nv_pci_remove/nvidia_exit_module to structurally close the UAF window for the rare genuine-timeout case, rather than relying on the worker self-exiting via the C5 sink before text-free?

risk.topic: SH-3: residual UAF risk after the 1200ms budget fix (apnex.22) on the rmmod/remove path, and the SH-3 live-test design
findings: BUDGET-CLOSES-CLOSE-PATH (confirmed). The 1200ms budget logic is sound *for the close path*. SH-1 measured rm_shutdown_adapter at 600-612ms (n=3, rock-solid; rm_disable ~12ms). 600 < 1200, so wait_for_completion_timeout (nv.c:2251) returns jiffies_left>0, the wrapper logs 'completed within budget', the worker has already run complete(&w->done) and dropped its ref, NO leak, NO sink-set, NO detached worker. The close path is converted from 'leak+race' to 'wait+clean'. f40b_fires stayed 1, state=healthy across all 3 SH-1 reps.

BUDGET DOES NOT *STRUCTURALLY* CLOSE THE rmmod-PATH UAF — IT ONLY RAISES THE BAR. The fix is purely a latency-margin bet (1200ms > measured 600ms close-path), not an architectural fix. If rm_shutdown_adapter on the *rmmod* path ever exceeds 1200ms, the wrapper times out (nv.c:2259-2289), calls rm_cleanup_gpu_lost_state, and LEAKS the worker on system_long_wq (nv.c:2291 comment 'worker still has 1 if running'). On rmmod that detached worker then races module-unload -> the UAF returns. The rmmod-path latency is UNMEASURED (SH-1 was close-path only, by design, nv.c trigger note 'Trigger (close-path, not rmmod)'). So the residual depends on an unmeasured number.

NO MODULE-REFERENCE PROTECTION EXISTS. The atomic_t refcount in struct nv_f40b_shutdown_work (nv.c:2185, atomic_set 2, dec_and_test->kfree at nv.c:2190) protects ONLY the heap allocation 'w' from being freed while the worker still touches it. It does NOT take a module reference. There is no try_module_get / module_put / __module_get anywhere in nv.c (only THIS_MODULE on file_operations.owner at nv.c:255, unrelated). So a leaked worker running w->rm_call (a function pointer INTO nvidia.ko text) does NOT prevent nvidia.ko text from being unmapped. If the worker is still in rm_shutdown_adapter when module_exit completes, it executes freed text -> UAF/oops.

NOTHING DRAINS system_long_wq BEFORE THE MODULE TEXT IS FREED. Grep of nv.c + nv-pci.c for flush_work/cancel_work_sync/flush_workqueue/flush_scheduled_work/destroy_workqueue/drain_workqueue finds ZERO hits for the A7 worker. The only cancel_work_sync is inside tb_egpu_recover_stop (A3, nv-pci.c:2408) for a DIFFERENT work item. The unload ordering is: nvidia_exit_module (nv.c:1047) -> nv_drivers_exit -> nv_pci_unregister_driver -> pci_unregister_driver -> (per-device) nv_pci_remove -> nv_pci_remove_helper(true) -> nv_shutdown_adapter -> nv_f40b_shutdown_bounded. The wrapper's own wait_for_completion_timeout is the ONLY synchronization; on TIMEOUT it deliberately stops waiting and returns. After nv_pci_remove returns, nv_module_exit/rm_shutdown_rm/module text free proceed with the leaked worker potentially still live. system_long_wq is a SHARED kernel workqueue that is never destroyed by this module, so it provides no unload barrier.

PRODUCTION DEPLOY-UNINSTALL DOES REACH THE A7 rmmod-PATH WRAP (when persistence is engaged). nv_pci_remove_helper reaches nv_shutdown_adapter via TWO paths: (1) nv_stop_device at nv-pci.c:2534 (which calls nv_shutdown_adapter only in its non-persistent branch, nv.c:2397; persistent branch at nv.c:2392 calls rm_disable only), and (2) the DIRECT call at nv-pci.c:2556 gated by (NV_FLAG_PERSISTENT_SW_STATE || NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED). The production DaemonSet runs nvidia-smi -pm 1, so NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED stays set across LAST-CLOSE and the direct nv-pci.c:2556 call DOES invoke nv_shutdown_adapter -> A7 rm_shutdown_adapter wrap on every persistence-engaged rmmod. This is exactly the Test-B-vs-Test-B-prime distinction: Test B (no persistence) fast-passed in 100ms because NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED was already cleared so rmmod SKIPPED nv_shutdown_adapter; the persistence-engaged production path does NOT skip it. So a normal persistence-engaged deploy-uninstall is a genuine, low-risk exerciser of the A7 rmmod-path wrap.

THE ORIGINAL 20:52 'WEDGE' IS NOT CONFIRMED TO BE THIS UAF — AND THE FORENSICS REPORT EXPLICITLY DISCLAIMS A7. The a7-deploy-wedge-2026-05-29/FORENSICS-REPORT.md concludes A7 was NOT loaded that boot (image never imported to k3s containerd) and the freeze fired ~10s AFTER rmmod logged its canonical last line ('Unregistered Nvlink Core' = nv_pci_remove completed). That report's leading hypothesis was a generic rmmod-path F40 MMIO hang in nv_shutdown_adapter, NOT a worker-vs-unload UAF (A7 wasn't even present). So the 20:52 event is consistent with EITHER an un-bounded MMIO hang (pre-A7 baseline, since A7 wasn't loaded) OR nothing GPU-related. It is NOT evidence for the H-SH5 UAF specifically. The UAF is a code-path hazard that is currently SPECULATIVE on real hardware (ledger H-SH5: 'never crashed, only not-yet-proven-safe'; 'No D-state zombie accumulation after ~5 rmmods' is the only positive evidence, and that was on the close path / pre-budget-fix).

WHY rmmod-PATH LATENCY COULD EXCEED THE CLOSE-PATH 600ms (the residual-risk rationale). The close path (nv_stop_device on LAST-CLOSE) and the rmmod path (pci_unregister_driver -> remove) reach the SAME rm_shutdown_adapter, but the surrounding teardown state differs: on rmmod the device is being surprise-removed/unbound, rm_notify_gpu_removal + rm_check_for_gpu_surprise_removal (nv-pci.c:2460/2512) and rm_disable_gpu_state_persistence (nv-pci.c:2554) run first, and the GSP shutdown handshake the worker busy-polls (SH-1 H-SH1 mechanism) may take longer or never converge if the TB link/GSP is in a different state than a clean close. 600ms close-path latency is NOT a safe proxy for rmmod-path latency. This is precisely the gap SH-3 must measure before declaring the budget sufficient.

/root/open-gpu-kernel-modules/kernel-open/nvidia/nv-pci.c:2529-2534 (nv_stop_device on remove path, init_on_probe-gated)
/root/open-gpu-kernel-modules/kernel-open/nvidia/nv-pci.c:2408 (tb_egpu_recover_stop cancel_work_sync — A3 work item, NOT the A7 worker)

risk.uaf_verdict: A genuine rmmod-path timeout (worker leaked + module unloads while worker still in rm_shutdown_adapter) IS A REAL use-after-free. The leaked worker holds NO module reference (no try_module_get; the atomic refcount guards only the kfree'd 'w' struct), and nothing on the unload path (nvidia_exit_module -> nv_pci_unregister_driver -> nv_module_exit) flushes or cancels the system_long_wq work item before nvidia.ko text is freed. So if the worker is still executing rm_call (a pointer into module text) when module unload completes, it runs freed code = UAF/oops. This is architecturally real and currently UNGUARDED. WHETHER the 1200ms budget CLOSES it: on the CLOSE path, YES — 600ms < 1200ms means the wrapper waits and the worker completes cleanly, no leak, so no UAF (SH-1 confirms this, converting close-path from leak+race to wait+clean). On the RMMOD path it is a LATENCY BET, not a structural close: the budget closes the UAF ONLY IF rmmod-path rm_shutdown_adapter also completes < 1200ms, which is UNMEASURED (SH-1 was close-path only). RESIDUAL = the rmmod-path latency tail above 1200ms, of unknown probability. IMPORTANT CAVEAT: the 20:52 forensics explicitly found A7 was NOT loaded that boot, so the historical 'wedge' is NOT proof of this UAF — the UAF is presently a code-path hazard with only one positive datapoint (no D-state zombies after ~5 close-path rmmods), not a reproduced field failure. RECOMMENDATION: ship a remove-path-specific structural guard in addition to the budget (DESIGN FIX, see open_questions) so the rmmod path never depends on the latency bet; then SH-3 is a confirmation test, not a load-bearing safety gate.
open_questions: DESIGN FIX (recommended, regardless of budget): make A7 use a remove/rmmod-path-specific strategy that does NOT rely on leak-on-timeout, because on unload the leak is unsafe by construction. Three options, in preference order: (1) On the remove path, after the timeout-and-sink-set, call flush_work(&w->work) or cancel_work_sync(&w->work) before nv_f40b_shutdown_bounded returns — the sink (rm_cleanup_gpu_lost_state) makes the worker's next sink-aware MMIO fail-fast and exit, so the flush should return quickly; this guarantees the worker is gone before module text can be freed, converting the residual UAF into at-worst a bounded extra wait. (2) Have the worker take try_module_get(THIS_MODULE) before the rm_call and module_put after, so the module refcount is non-zero while any worker runs and rmmod blocks (returns EBUSY) rather than unmapping live text — simplest correctness guarantee, at the cost of rmmod being deferred. (3) A dedicated, module-owned workqueue (alloc_workqueue) that nv_module_exit destroy_workqueue()s as an unload barrier. Recommend option (1) gated on a 'this is the remove path' flag (or unconditionally, since flush-after-sink is cheap), because it is the smallest change that removes the latency bet entirely and matches the project's funnel-over-per-site discipline. The budget bump and this guard are complementary: budget makes the common case 'wait+clean' (no timeout at all), the guard makes the rare timeout case safe-on-unload.

SH-3 LIVE-TEST DESIGN (recommended test ladder, abort criteria, recovery). PRECONDITION GATE: aorus.22 (1200ms) deployed + verified (cat /sys/module/nvidia/version; read NVreg_TbEgpuShutdownTimeoutMs=1200), BAR1=32GiB confirmed via sysfs BEFORE any RPC tool, clean substrate, persistence state known. RUNG 1 (LOW RISK — do this first; it is what every deploy already does): observe a NORMAL persistence-engaged deploy-uninstall (kubectl exec pod -- /entrypoint.sh uninstall, then the DaemonSet's rmmod). Because production runs nvidia-smi -pm 1, NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED stays set so nv_pci_remove_helper:2556 DOES call nv_shutdown_adapter -> A7 rm_shutdown_adapter wrap on the rmmod path (Test-B-prime distinction confirmed in source). MEASURE: journalctl -k grep 'tb_egpu [F40b]: rm_shutdown_adapter (scheduled|completed within budget|timed out)' + the 'Unregistered Nvlink Core' last-line + tb_egpu_f40b_fires counter delta. SUCCESS CRITERION: 'rm_shutdown_adapter completed within budget' appears (NO 'timed out'), fires counter does NOT increment, rmmod returns rc=0, host alive. Repeat n>=3 across separate reboots to get the rmmod-path latency distribution (sec-resolution from journal is enough to confirm <1200ms; if a finer number is needed, that is an SH-2-style instrumented ktime add, not RUNG 2). This rung alone likely RESOLVES SH-3: if rmmod-path latency clusters near the 600ms close-path figure with zero timeouts over n>=3, the budget is empirically sufficient and the deliberate-leak test (RUNG 2) is UNNECESSARY. RUNG 2 (HIGH RISK — only if RUNG 1 shows a timeout, or if a worst-case UAF reproduction is explicitly wanted): deliberately set NVreg_TbEgpuShutdownTimeoutMs to a too-short value (e.g. 100ms, below the 600ms floor) to FORCE the leak+race on a persistence-engaged rmmod and observe whether the leaked-worker-vs-unload race actually wedges/oops. This reproduces the suspected 20:52-class UAF on purpose -> EXPECT host wedge/oops -> reboot. Arm sysrq + fsync'd journalctl -kf tee + pre-forked reboot shell BEFORE this rung. RUNG 2 is the falsification test for 'is the UAF real on THIS hardware', but it is destructive and should be run at most n=1, and ONLY after the design guard above is NOT yet shipped (once the guard ships, RUNG 2 can no longer leak, so run RUNG 2 on a guard-LESS build if you want the negative-control datapoint).

SH-3 ABORT CRITERIA: (RUNG 1) journal goes SILENT after 'rm_shutdown_adapter scheduled to bounded worker' with no matching completed/timed-out within ~5s -> deadlock/wedge -> reboot; timeout-wrapped rmmod (timeout 20 rmmod nvidia) returns 124 OR rmmod in uninterruptible D-state -> wedged -> reboot; any oops/BUG/'BUG: unable to handle' or 'Oops' in dmesg referencing nvidia or system_long_wq/events_long after rmmod -> the UAF fired -> capture + reboot; scheduler-wide journal silence (systemd/k3s timers stop) -> immediate reboot. (RUNG 2) same, plus: treat ANY post-rmmod oops as the EXPECTED positive result, capture and reboot. Cap any forced-timeout experiment at the ledger's 10s thermal ceiling (the busy-poll pegs one CPU core; project_h21_cpu_thermals).

SH-3 RECOVERY: on any wedge -> host reboot (the chip substrate stays structurally fine, BAR1 32GiB, so a clean reboot restores baseline and the DaemonSet re-binds). Passive reads only on a suspected-wedged chip (lspci, sysfs, dmesg) — NO nvidia-smi / no MMIO-issuing observability post-wedge (feedback_no_rpc_observability_on_broken_bar1). Do NOT run the userspace rmmod->TB->fix-bar1->modprobe loop to un-wedge a deadlocked kernel.

RECOMMENDATION SYNTHESIS: SH-3 is likely RESOLVABLE by RUNG 1 alone (low-risk, n>=3 persistence-engaged deploy-uninstalls measuring rmmod-path rm_shutdown_adapter latency + confirming 'completed within budget' with zero timeouts). RUNG 2 (deliberate too-short-budget leak) is NOT recommended as a routine step — it deliberately reproduces a host-wedging UAF; reserve it as a one-shot falsification only if RUNG 1 surprises with a timeout, or as a negative-control on a guard-less build. STRONGEST recommendation: ship the remove-path flush_work/cancel_work_sync (or try_module_get) DESIGN GUARD so the rmmod path is structurally UAF-safe independent of latency, demoting SH-3 from a safety gate to a confirmation test.

FOLLOW-ON: SH-2 (eBPF on osDevReadReg032) characterizes WHY ~600ms and whether rmmod-path latency differs mechanistically from close-path — relevant because a longer rmmod-path GSP handshake is the specific scenario that would blow the 1200ms budget. Consider folding a 'rmmod-path vs close-path latency comparison' into SH-2's scope.

synth.lifecycle_model:
DEFINITIVE WORKER-VS-UNLOAD LIFECYCLE (nv.c:2179-2293, confirmed against source).

Setup: nv_f40b_shutdown_bounded kzalloc's struct nv_f40b_shutdown_work w (embeds work_struct + completion + rm_call ptr + sp + nv + atomic refcount), atomic_set(refcount,2) "one for caller, one for worker", queue_work(system_long_wq, &w->work), then wait_for_completion_timeout(&w->done, msecs_to_jiffies(timeout_ms)). The struct is HEAP; the worker fn (nv_f40b_shutdown_worker) and the target (rm_call -> rm_shutdown_adapter) are MODULE TEXT.

PATH A — COMPLETE-WITHIN-BUDGET (clean). Worker runs w->rm_call (~600ms per SH-1, n=3 rock-solid), then complete(&w->done) at nv.c:2200, then work_put (refcount 2->1) at nv.c:2201. wait_for_completion_timeout returns jiffies_left>0 AFTER complete() ran, so rm_shutdown_adapter has provably returned before the wrapper wakes. Wrapper logs "completed within budget", then work_put (1->0 -> kfree). No leak, no sink set, no detached worker, no module-unload hazard. The only race is the benign refcount race over which put is last (worker@2201 vs wrapper@2291) — correctly serialized; complete() at 2200 is sequenced before the worker's own put, so the wrapper never proceeds while rm_call is still running.

PATH B — TIMEOUT (leak). wait_for_completion_timeout returns 0. Wrapper takes the else branch (nv.c:2259-2291): logs "worker leaked, will exit when MMIO fails-fast post-sink-set", calls C5 sink rm_cleanup_gpu_lost_state(...DETECTOR_AER_FATAL), counts the fire via nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired_teardown(), then work_put drops ONLY the caller ref (2->1, struct NOT freed — comment "worker still has 1 if running"). Wrapper returns into nv_shutdown_adapter and teardown proceeds. The worker is STILL executing rm_call=rm_shutdown_adapter (module text) holding the last ref; when it eventually returns it does complete() (no waiter) + work_put (1->0 -> kfree).

WHAT EACH MEANS FOR THE MODULE: Path A is unconditionally safe on any path including rmmod. Path B is safe on the CLOSE path (process keeps running, text stays mapped) but UNSAFE on the rmmod/.remove path: the worker holds NO module reference (verified: zero try_module_get/module_put in nv.c/nv-pci.c for the F40b path), and the unload path has NO flush/cancel of system_long_wq (verified: the only cancel_work_sync is nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:873 on A3's reset_work, a different item on system_wq). system_long_wq is a kernel-owned shared workqueue, so it provides no module-scoped unload barrier. Thus on a Path-B rmmod, free_module() can unmap nvidia.ko .text while the leaked worker still executes rm_shutdown_adapter, and nv_pci_remove_helper additionally NV_KFREE(nvl) while the worker still dereferences w->nv/w->sp — a double UAF (freed text + freed state).
synth.budget_fix_closes_race:
PARTIALLY — the 1200ms budget closes the CLOSE-path race structurally but only narrows (does not close) the RMMOD-path race; it is a latency-margin bet, not an architectural fix.

CLOSE PATH: CLOSED. SH-1 measured rm_shutdown_adapter at 600-612ms (n=3) and rm_disable ~12ms. 600 << 1200, so wait_for_completion_timeout returns jiffies_left>0 and the wrapper waits for the worker to fully complete (Path A) — converting the old 200ms "leak+race" into "wait+clean". No leak fires, no detached worker, no UAF. The old 200ms guillotine guaranteed a leak (200 < 600); the 1200ms budget eliminates the leak on every healthy completion.

RMMOD PATH: NOT structurally closed. The fix only helps IF rmmod-path rm_shutdown_adapter also completes < 1200ms, which is UNMEASURED — SH-1 instrumented the close path only (trigger note: "close-path, not rmmod"). The rmmod path reaches the same rm_shutdown_adapter but under different surrounding state: surprise-removal/unbind, rm_notify_gpu_removal + rm_check_for_gpu_surprise_removal + rm_disable_gpu_state_persistence run first, and the GSP shutdown handshake the worker busy-polls may take longer or never converge if the TB link/GSP is in a worse state than a clean close. So 600ms close-path latency is NOT a safe proxy for the rmmod tail.

RESIDUAL RISK: if rm_shutdown_adapter on rmmod exceeds 1200ms (e.g. PCIe Completion Timeout MMIO hang on a recovered eGPU chip — precisely the F40 scenario A7 exists to survive), the timeout branch fires, leaks the worker on system_long_wq, and the worker races module-unload -> the double UAF (freed text + freed nvl) returns. Probability is unknown (unmeasured rmmod-path tail above 1200ms). Net: the budget raises the bar from "leak on every rmmod" (200ms) to "leak only on a genuine >1200ms hang", but the consequence of crossing the bar on rmmod is unchanged (UAF), because no module-pin or unload-drain backstops it.
synth.needs_design_fix:
YES — A7 needs a remove-path-specific structural guard beyond the budget, so the rmmod path never depends on the latency bet. RECOMMENDED (preference order):

(1) PREFERRED — bounded flush after sink-set on the remove path. In the timeout branch, after rm_cleanup_gpu_lost_state sets the C5 sink, call flush_work(&w->work) (or cancel_work_sync(&w->work)) before nv_f40b_shutdown_bounded returns. The sink makes the worker's next sink-aware MMIO fail-fast and exit, so the flush should return quickly; it GUARANTEES the worker has left module text before nv_shutdown_adapter returns and unload can free .text/nvl. This converts the residual UAF into at-worst a bounded additional wait, matches the project's funnel-over-per-site discipline (single funnel point in the shared helper), and keeps the leak-and-fast-fail design while removing its only unsafe consequence. Risk: if the sink is NOT consulted on the wedged MMIO path (SH-2 open question on osDevReadReg032), flush could re-introduce an unbounded block — so gate it with its own bounded retry/timeout, or accept that this case already wedges today.

(2) ALTERNATIVE — try_module_get(THIS_MODULE) in the worker before rm_call + module_put after. Non-zero module refcount while any worker runs makes rmmod return EBUSY/defer instead of unmapping live text — simplest correctness guarantee. Cost: rmmod is deferred while a worker is stuck (kubectl uninstall could hang), which may be worse operationally than a bounded flush for the DaemonSet lifecycle.

(3) ALTERNATIVE — module-owned workqueue via alloc_workqueue that nv_module_exit destroy_workqueue()s as an unload barrier. Cleanest architecturally but the largest change; destroy_workqueue is also an unbounded wait on a wedged worker, same caveat as (1).

The budget bump and the guard are COMPLEMENTARY, not redundant: the budget makes the common case "wait+clean" (no timeout at all, so the guard rarely runs); the guard makes the rare genuine-timeout case safe-on-unload. Ship the guard so SH-3 becomes a confirmation test rather than a load-bearing safety gate. The guard need not be remove-path-ONLY — flush-after-sink is cheap and correct on the close path too — but it is MANDATORY on the remove path.
synth.wedge_2052_verdict:
The 20:52 wedge is NOT the leaked-worker UAF, on two independent source/forensics-confirmed grounds, and the original "rm_shutdown_adapter MMIO hang" attribution is itself now undermined.

(1) PRECONDITION ABSENT: A7 (the patch that schedules the leaked worker) was NOT in the 20:52 build. The kernel-log build banner is aorus.18-f40b (595.71.05, root@nvidia-driver-injector-s7bxd, Fri May 29 09:46:52 UTC 2026), which carries only the A6 OPEN-path wrapper. The log shows A6 open-path F40b lines and ZERO shutdown-path F40b lines (no "scheduled to bounded worker", no "completed within budget", no "timed out"). A7's first deploy was aorus.19 at ~21:35, ~40 min AFTER the wedge. The FORENSICS-REPORT.md independently concludes A7's image was never imported to k3s containerd that boot. So the 20:52 rmmod ran the VANILLA synchronous nv_shutdown_adapter — no worker was ever queued, none could be leaked. The UAF mechanism is mechanistically inapplicable to this event; it can only apply to A7-bearing builds (aorus.19+).

(2) TIMING MISMATCH even hypothetically: a 200ms-budget leaked worker would finish rm_shutdown at ~rmmod+600ms and race module-text-free within <1s of the 20:52:18 "Unregistered Nvlink Core" line — firing a UAF panic at ~20:52:18-19, NOT at the observed freeze at 20:52:28 (+10s, last line "cni0: port 17(vethe092bfe0) entered forwarding state"). The +10s gap aligns precisely with the NEW pod's container-setup phase (BPF program loads, veth/cni0 creation, netfilter/iptables-restore, avahi mDNS).

BEST EXPLANATION: a SEPARATE chip-touching event during the new pod's container setup at +8-10s, on a chip left in a bad post-rmmod substrate state — i.e. an async PCI/AER scan, a TB-tunnel poll, a modprobe/PCI-rescan, or a container PCI-sysfs read hitting a chip that the vanilla synchronous teardown left in an F40-precondition state. Consistent with the broader F40/surprise-removal/modprobe-spin classes in project memory, NOT a teardown-worker UAF. Importantly, SH-1 (rm_shutdown_adapter completes in ~600ms, does not hang) also undermines the report's own "rm_shutdown_adapter MMIO hang" leading hypothesis — so the 20:52 attribution is doubly-undermined and should be reframed: A7's Purpose/Provenance prose that cites the 20:52 forensics as A7's originating attestation OVERSTATES the link and warrants a provenance correction (the A7 v1.2 header already flags reframing).
synth.sh3_plan:
SH-3 is RESOLVABLE primarily by code analysis plus a LOW-RISK Rung-1 confirmation; the destructive Rung-2 leak-reproduction is NOT recommended as routine. Ladder:

PRECONDITION GATE: aorus.22 (1200ms) deployed + verified (cat /sys/module/nvidia/version; read NVreg_TbEgpuShutdownTimeoutMs=1200). BAR1=32GiB confirmed via SYSFS before any RPC/MMIO tool (feedback_no_rpc_observability_on_broken_bar1). Clean substrate, persistence state known, sysrq armed, fsync'd journalctl -kf | tee to disk, pre-forked reboot shell.

RUNG 1 (LOW RISK — what every deploy already does). Observe n>=3 NORMAL persistence-engaged deploy-uninstalls across separate reboots (entrypoint uninstall, then DaemonSet rmmod). Because production runs nvidia-smi -pm 1, NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED stays set so nv_pci_remove_helper (nv-pci.c:2556) DOES call nv_shutdown_adapter -> A7 rm_shutdown_adapter wrap on the rmmod path (verified gating: the direct call is gated on NV_FLAG_PERSISTENT_SW_STATE||NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED). This is the Test-B-prime distinction — Test B without persistence fast-passes in ~100ms because NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED is already cleared and rmmod SKIPS nv_shutdown_adapter. MEASURE via journal: grep "tb_egpu [F40b]: rm_shutdown_adapter (scheduled|completed within budget|timed out)", the "Unregistered Nvlink Core" last line, and the tb_egpu_f40b_fires counter delta. SUCCESS: "completed within budget" appears, NO "timed out", fires counter does NOT increment, rmmod rc=0, host alive. If rmmod-path latency clusters near the 600ms close-path figure with zero timeouts over n>=3, the budget is empirically sufficient and Rung 2 is UNNECESSARY — SH-3 resolves here.

RUNG 2 (HIGH RISK — only if Rung 1 shows a timeout, or if a worst-case UAF reproduction is explicitly wanted, AND only on a guard-LESS build). Deliberately set NVreg_TbEgpuShutdownTimeoutMs below the 600ms floor (e.g. 100ms) to FORCE the leak+race on a persistence-engaged rmmod and observe whether the leaked-worker-vs-unload race actually wedges/oops. EXPECT host wedge/oops -> reboot. At most n=1, destructive. Cap any forced-timeout spin at the ~10s thermal ceiling (busy-poll pegs a core, project_h21_cpu_thermals).

ABORT CRITERIA: journal silent after "scheduled to bounded worker" with no matching completed/timed-out within ~5s -> wedge -> reboot; timeout-wrapped rmmod (timeout 20 rmmod nvidia) returns 124 OR rmmod in D-state -> reboot; any Oops/BUG/"unable to handle" referencing nvidia or system_long_wq/events_long after rmmod -> the UAF fired, capture + reboot; scheduler-wide journal silence -> immediate reboot. (Rung 2: treat any post-rmmod oops as the EXPECTED positive, capture + reboot.)

RECOVERY: on any wedge -> host reboot (chip substrate stays structurally fine, BAR1 32GiB, clean reboot restores baseline, DaemonSet re-binds). PASSIVE reads only on a suspected-wedged chip (lspci, sysfs, dmesg) — NO nvidia-smi / no MMIO-issuing observability post-wedge. Do NOT run the userspace rmmod->TB->fix-bar1->modprobe loop to un-wedge a deadlocked kernel.

STRONGEST RECOMMENDATION: ship the remove-path flush_work/cancel_work_sync (or try_module_get) DESIGN GUARD first; that makes the rmmod path structurally UAF-safe independent of latency and demotes SH-3 from a safety gate to a Rung-1 confirmation. Fold a "rmmod-path vs close-path latency comparison" into SH-2's scope (eBPF on osDevReadReg032) since a longer rmmod-path GSP handshake is the specific scenario that would blow the 1200ms budget.
